From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British patriotic song

For other uses, see [God Save the King (disambiguation)](/wiki/God_Save_the_King_(disambiguation) "God Save the King (disambiguation)") and [God Save the Queen (disambiguation)](/wiki/God_Save_the_Queen_(disambiguation) "God Save the Queen (disambiguation)").

"**God Save the King**" (also known as "**God Save the Queen**" when the [monarch is female](/wiki/Queen_regnant "Queen regnant")) is the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem") of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom").[[6]](#cite_note-royal_family-7) It is one of the [two national anthems](/wiki/National_anthems_of_New_Zealand "National anthems of New Zealand") of [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand")[[1]](#cite_note-NZ-1) and the [royal anthem](/wiki/Royal_anthem "Royal anthem") of the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man"),[[7]](#cite_note-8) [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"),[[8]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10) [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") and some other [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realms "Commonwealth realms").[[3]](#cite_note-Factbook-3) The author of the song is unknown and it may originate as a [plainchant](/wiki/Plainchant "Plainchant"), but an attribution to the composer [John Bull](/wiki/John_Bull_(composer) "John Bull (composer)") has sometimes been made.

Beyond its first verse, which is consistent, "God Save the King" has many historic and extant versions. Since its first publication, different verses have been added and taken away and, even today, different publications include various selections of verses in various orders.[[10]](#cite_note-11) In general, only one verse is sung. Sometimes two verses are sung and, on certain occasions, three.[[6]](#cite_note-royal_family-7)

The entire composition is the [musical salute](/wiki/Honors_music "Honors music") for the [British monarch](/wiki/British_monarch "British monarch") and their [royal consort](/wiki/Consort_of_the_British_monarch "Consort of the British monarch"),[[11]](#cite_note-12) while other members of the [British royal family](/wiki/British_royal_family "British royal family") who are entitled to royal salute (such as the [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales "Prince of Wales"), along with his spouse) receive just the first six [bars](/wiki/Bar_(music) "Bar (music)"). The first six bars also form all or part of the [viceregal](/wiki/Viceroy "Viceroy") salute in some Commonwealth realms other than the UK (e.g., in Canada, [governors general](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") and lieutenant governors at official events are saluted with the first six bars of "God Save the King" followed by the first four and last four bars of "[O Canada](/wiki/O_Canada "O Canada")"), as well as the salute given to governors of [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories").

In countries not part of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), the tune of "God Save the King" has provided the basis for various patriotic songs, ones generally connected with royal ceremony.[[12]](#cite_note-13) The melody is used for the national anthem of [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein"), "*[Oben am jungen Rhein](/wiki/Oben_am_jungen_Rhein "Oben am jungen Rhein")*"; the royal anthem of [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"), "*[Kongesangen](/wiki/Kongesangen "Kongesangen")*"; and the American patriotic song "[My Country, 'Tis of Thee](/wiki/My_Country,_%27Tis_of_Thee "My Country, 'Tis of Thee")" (also known as "America"). The melody was also used for the national anthem "*[Heil dir im Siegerkranz](/wiki/Heil_dir_im_Siegerkranz "Heil dir im Siegerkranz")*" ("Hail to thee in the Victor's Crown") of the [Kingdom of Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") from 1795 until 1918; as the anthem of the [German Emperor](/wiki/German_Emperor "German Emperor") from 1871 to 1918; as "[The Prayer of Russians](/wiki/The_Prayer_of_Russians "The Prayer of Russians")", the imperial anthem of the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), from 1816 to 1833; and as the national anthem of [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), "*[Rufst du, mein Vaterland](/wiki/Rufst_du,_mein_Vaterland "Rufst du, mein Vaterland")*", from the 1840s until 1961.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The text first appeared in England in the late 1590s, with the publication of [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare")'s play *[Richard III](/wiki/Richard_III_(play) "Richard III (play)")*. In Act IV, Scene I, [Lady Anne](/wiki/Anne_Neville "Anne Neville") says to [Queen Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_Woodville "Elizabeth Woodville"): "Were red-hot steel to sear me to the brains! Anointed let me be with deadly venom, And die ere men can say 'God save the Queen.'"[[13]](#cite_note-14)

In *[The Oxford Companion to Music](/wiki/The_Oxford_Companion_to_Music "The Oxford Companion to Music")*, [Percy Scholes](/wiki/Percy_Scholes "Percy Scholes") points out the similarities to an early [plainsong](/wiki/Plainsong "Plainsong") melody, although the rhythm is very distinctly that of a [galliard](/wiki/Galliard "Galliard"),[[14]](#cite_note-15) and he gives examples of several such dance tunes that bear a striking resemblance to "God Save the King". Scholes quotes a keyboard piece by [John Bull](/wiki/John_Bull_(composer) "John Bull (composer)") (1619) which has some similarities to the modern tune, depending on the placing of [accidentals](/wiki/Accidental_(music) "Accidental (music)") which at that time were unwritten in certain cases and left to the discretion of the player (see *[musica ficta](/wiki/Musica_ficta "Musica ficta")*). He also points to several pieces by [Henry Purcell](/wiki/Henry_Purcell "Henry Purcell"), one of which includes the opening notes of the modern tune, setting the words "God Save the King". Nineteenth-century scholars and commentators mention the widespread belief that an old Scots carol, "Remember O Thou Man", was the source of the tune.[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17)

The first published version that resembles the present song appeared in 1744, with no title but the heading "For two voices", in an anthology originally named *Harmonia Britannia* but changed after only a few copies had been printed to *Thesaurus Musicus*.[[17]](#cite_note-18) When the [Jacobite](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism") pretender [Charles Edward Stuart](/wiki/Charles_Edward_Stuart "Charles Edward Stuart") led the [1745 rising](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1745 "Jacobite rising of 1745"), the song spread among those loyal to King [George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain"). The tune published in *[The Gentleman's Magazine](/wiki/The_Gentleman%27s_Magazine "The Gentleman's Magazine")* in 1745 departs from that used today at several points, one as early as the first bar, but is otherwise clearly a strong relative of the contemporary anthem. It was recorded as being sung in London theatres in 1745, with, for example, [Thomas Arne](/wiki/Thomas_Arne "Thomas Arne") writing a setting of the tune for the [Drury Lane Theatre](/wiki/Theatre_Royal,_Drury_Lane "Theatre Royal, Drury Lane").

Scholes' analysis includes mention of "untenable" and "doubtful" claims, as well as "an American misattribution". Some of these are:

* [James Oswald](/wiki/James_Oswald_(composer) "James Oswald (composer)") was a possible author of the *Thesaurus Musicus*, so may have played a part in the history of the song, but is not a strong enough candidate to be cited as the composer of the tune.
* [Henry Carey](/wiki/Henry_Carey_(writer) "Henry Carey (writer)"): Scholes refutes this attribution: first on the grounds that Carey himself never made such a claim; second, when the claim was made by Carey's son (in 1795), it was in support of a request for a pension from the British Government; and third, the younger Carey claimed that his father, who died in 1743, had written parts of the song in 1745. It has also been claimed that the work was first publicly performed by Carey during a dinner in 1740 in honour of Admiral [Edward Vernon](/wiki/Edward_Vernon "Edward Vernon"), who had captured the Spanish harbour of [Porto Bello](/wiki/Porto_Bello,_Panama "Porto Bello, Panama") (then in the [Viceroyalty of New Granada](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_New_Granada "Viceroyalty of New Granada"), now in [Panama](/wiki/Panama "Panama")) during the [War of Jenkins' Ear](/wiki/War_of_Jenkins%27_Ear "War of Jenkins' Ear").

Scholes recommends the attribution "traditional" or "traditional; earliest known version by John Bull (1562–1628)". The *[English Hymnal](/wiki/English_Hymnal "English Hymnal")* (musical editor [Ralph Vaughan Williams](/wiki/Ralph_Vaughan_Williams "Ralph Vaughan Williams")) gives no attribution, stating merely "17th or 18th cent."[[18]](#cite_note-19)

## Use in the United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Use in the United Kingdom")]

[![Poster of blimp above London at nighttime, with the text "It is far better to face the bullets than to be killed at home by a bomb. Join the army at once & help to stop an air raid. God save the King".](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/It_is_far_better_to_face_the_bullets.jpg/250px-It_is_far_better_to_face_the_bullets.jpg)](/wiki/File:It_is_far_better_to_face_the_bullets.jpg)

The phrase "God Save the King" in use as a rallying cry to the support of the monarch and the UK's forces during the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War")

[![A group of men in World War I uniforms of the Royal Marines and Royal Navy, some of whom are holding metal pails, standing adjacent to a wooden tub inscribed with "God Save the King". A man scoops liquid from the tub (the rum ration) and pours it into the metal pails, as a Petty Officer looks on from a logbook. They are standing belowdecks on a ship with a low ceiling, with pipes and cables snaking overhead.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/The_Royal_Navy_during_the_First_World_War_Q17966.jpg/250px-The_Royal_Navy_during_the_First_World_War_Q17966.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Royal_Navy_during_the_First_World_War_Q17966.jpg)

Royal Marines and [bluejackets](/wiki/Naval_rating "Naval rating") aboard [HMS *Royal Oak*](/wiki/HMS_Royal_Oak_(08) "HMS Royal Oak (08)") being served their [rum rations](/wiki/Rum_ration "Rum ration") from a rum tub inscribed with "God save the King" (1916)

Like many aspects of [British constitutional](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constitution of the United Kingdom") life, "God Save the King" derives its official status from custom and use, not from [royal proclamation](/wiki/Royal_proclamation "Royal proclamation") or [act of Parliament](/wiki/Act_of_Parliament "Act of Parliament").[[19]](#cite_note-20) The variation in the UK of the lyrics to "God Save the King" is the oldest among those currently used, and forms the basis on which all other versions used throughout the Commonwealth are formed; though, again, the words have varied over time.

England has no official [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem") of its own; "God Save the King" is treated as the English national anthem when England is represented at sporting events (though there are some exceptions to this rule, such as cricket where "[Jerusalem](/wiki/And_did_those_feet_in_ancient_time "And did those feet in ancient time")" is used). There is a movement to establish an English national anthem, with [William Blake](/wiki/William_Blake "William Blake")'s and [Hubert Parry](/wiki/Hubert_Parry "Hubert Parry")'s "Jerusalem" and [Edward Elgar](/wiki/Edward_Elgar "Edward Elgar")'s "[Land of Hope and Glory](/wiki/Land_of_Hope_and_Glory "Land of Hope and Glory")" among the top contenders. Wales has a *de facto* national anthem, "[Hen Wlad Fy Nhadau](/wiki/Hen_Wlad_Fy_Nhadau "Hen Wlad Fy Nhadau")" (Land of my Fathers) while Scotland uses unofficial anthems ("[Scotland the Brave](/wiki/Scotland_the_Brave "Scotland the Brave")" was traditionally used until the 1990s; since then, "[Flower of Scotland](/wiki/Flower_of_Scotland "Flower of Scotland")" is more commonly used), these anthems are used formally at state and national ceremonies as well as international sporting events such as [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") matches.[[20]](#cite_note-olympic_usage-21) On all occasions in Northern Ireland, "God Save the King" is still used as the official anthem.

In 2001, it was claimed that the phrase "No surrender" was occasionally sung in the bridge before "Send her victorious" by England football fans at matches.[[21]](#cite_note-BackCrabbe2001-22)[[22]](#cite_note-IndObit-23)

Since 2003, "God Save the King", considered an all-inclusive anthem for Great Britain and Northern Ireland, as well as other countries within the Commonwealth, has been dropped from the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"). Northern Irish athletes receive their gold medals to the tune of the "[Londonderry Air](/wiki/Londonderry_Air "Londonderry Air")", popularly known as "[Danny Boy](/wiki/Danny_Boy "Danny Boy")". In 2006, English winners heard Elgar's ["*Pomp and Circumstance March* No. 1"](/wiki/Pomp_and_Circumstance_Marches "Pomp and Circumstance Marches"), usually known as "Land of Hope and Glory",[[23]](#cite_note-24) but after a poll conducted by the [Commonwealth Games Council for England](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Council_for_England "Commonwealth Games Council for England") prior to the 2010 Games, "Jerusalem" was adopted as England's new Commonwealth Games anthem. In sports in which the UK competes as one nation, most notably as [Great Britain at the Olympics](/wiki/Great_Britain_at_the_Olympics "Great Britain at the Olympics"), the anthem is used to represent anyone or any team that comes from the United Kingdom.[[20]](#cite_note-olympic_usage-21)

### Lyrics in the UK

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Lyrics in the UK")]

First verse sung at the Royal Exchange in 2022

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Stratford-upon-Avon_Town_Hall_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7571824.jpg/250px-Stratford-upon-Avon_Town_Hall_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7571824.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stratford-upon-Avon_Town_Hall_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7571824.jpg)

[Stratford-upon-Avon Town Hall](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon_Town_Hall "Stratford-upon-Avon Town Hall") (built 1767), bearing the painted slogan, "God Save the King"

The phrase "God Save the King" is much older than the song, appearing, for instance, several times in the [King James Bible](/wiki/King_James_Bible "King James Bible").[[24]](#cite_note-25) [A text](/wiki/Zadok_the_Priest "Zadok the Priest") based on the [1st Book of Kings](/wiki/Books_of_Kings "Books of Kings") Chapter 1: verses 38–40, "And all the people rejoic'd, and said: God save the King! Long live the King! May the King live for ever, Amen", has been sung and proclaimed at every [coronation](/wiki/Coronation_of_the_British_monarch "Coronation of the British monarch") since that of [King Edgar](/wiki/Edgar_of_England "Edgar of England") in 973.[[25]](#cite_note-WestminsterAbbey1-26) Scholes says that as early as 1545, "God Save the King" was a [watchword](/wiki/Watchword "Watchword") of the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"), with the response being "Long to reign over us".[[26]](#cite_note-27)[[27]](#cite_note-28) He also notes that the prayer read in churches on anniversaries of the [Gunpowder Plot](/wiki/Gunpowder_Plot "Gunpowder Plot") includes words which might have formed part of the basis for the former standard verse "Scatter our enemies...assuage their malice and confound their devices".

In 1745, *The Gentleman's Magazine* published "God save our lord the king: A new song set for two voices", describing it "As sung at both Playhouses" (the Theatres Royal at [Drury Lane](/wiki/Theatre_Royal,_Drury_Lane "Theatre Royal, Drury Lane") and [Covent Garden](/wiki/Royal_Opera_House "Royal Opera House")).[[28]](#cite_note-gentsmag-29) Traditionally, the first performance was thought to have been in 1745, when it was sung in support of [King George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain") after his defeat at the [Battle of Prestonpans](/wiki/Battle_of_Prestonpans "Battle of Prestonpans") by the army of [Charles Edward Stuart](/wiki/Charles_Edward_Stuart "Charles Edward Stuart"), son of [James Francis Edward Stuart](/wiki/James_Francis_Edward_Stuart "James Francis Edward Stuart"), the [Jacobite](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism") claimant to the British throne.

It is sometimes claimed that, ironically, the song was originally sung in support of the Jacobite cause: the word "send" in the line "Send him victorious" could imply that the king was absent. However, the *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")* cites examples of "[God] send (a person) safe, victorious, etc." meaning "God grant that he may be safe, etc.". There are also examples of early 18th-century drinking glasses which are inscribed with a version of the words and were apparently intended for drinking the health of [King James II and VII](/wiki/King_James_II_and_VII "King James II and VII").

Scholes acknowledges these possibilities but argues that the same words were probably being used by both Jacobite and Hanoverian supporters and directed at their respective kings.[[29]](#cite_note-mwtppa-30)

In 1902, the musician [William Hayman Cummings](/wiki/William_Hayman_Cummings "William Hayman Cummings"), quoting mid-18th-century correspondence between [Charles Burney](/wiki/Charles_Burney "Charles Burney") and [Sir Joseph Banks](/wiki/Sir_Joseph_Banks "Sir Joseph Banks"), suggested that the words had been based on a Latin verse composed for King James II at the [Chapel Royal](/wiki/Chapel_Royal "Chapel Royal").

O Deus optime!  
Salvum nunc facito  
Regem nostrum;  
Sit laeta victoria,  
Comes et gloria,  
Salvum iam facito,  
Tu dominum.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

O most excellent God  
Save our King now;  
May joyous victory and glory  
Be his companions,  
Save our lord now.

#### Standard version in the United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Standard version in the United Kingdom")]

"God Save the King" performed with each of its three verses (originally released on a Victor Record phonograph c. 1910)

As the reigning monarch is currently [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"), the male version of the anthem is used.

**When the current monarch is male**

> God save our gracious King!  
> Long live our noble King!  
> God save the King!  
> Send him victorious,  
> Happy and glorious,  
> Long to reign over us,  
> God save the King!  
>   
> Thy choicest gifts in store,  
> On him be pleased to pour;  
> Long may he reign.  
> May he defend our laws,  
> And ever give us cause,  
> To sing with heart and voice,  
> God save the King![[6]](#cite_note-royal_family-7)

When the monarch of the time is female, "King" is replaced with "Queen" and all masculine pronouns are replaced with their feminine equivalents.

There is no definitive version of the lyrics. However, the version consisting of the two above verses has the best claim to be regarded as the "standard" British version as referenced on the Royal Family website.[[6]](#cite_note-royal_family-7)
The song with an additional verse appears not only in the 1745 *Gentleman's Magazine*, but also in publications such as *The Book of English Songs: From the Sixteenth to the Nineteenth Century* (1851),[[31]](#cite_note-32) *National Hymns: How They Are Written and How They Are Not Written* (1861),[[32]](#cite_note-33) *Household Book of Poetry* (1882),[[33]](#cite_note-34) and *[Hymns Ancient and Modern](/wiki/Hymns_Ancient_and_Modern "Hymns Ancient and Modern"), Revised Version* (1982).[[34]](#cite_note-35)

The same version with appears in publications including *[Scouting for Boys](/wiki/Scouting_for_Boys "Scouting for Boys")* (1908),[[35]](#cite_note-36) and on the Royal Family website.[[6]](#cite_note-royal_family-7)

In the UK, the first verse is typically sung alone, even on official occasions, although the second verse is sometimes sung in addition on certain occasions such as during the opening ceremonies of the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics"), [2012 Summer Paralympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Paralympics "2012 Summer Paralympics"), and the [2022 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2022_Commonwealth_Games "2022 Commonwealth Games") and usually at the [Last Night of the Proms](/wiki/Last_Night_of_the_Proms "Last Night of the Proms"). The second verse was also sung during the [coronation of King Charles III and Queen Camilla](/wiki/Coronation_of_Charles_III_and_Camilla "Coronation of Charles III and Camilla").

#### Standard version of the music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Standard version of the music")]

"God Save the Queen" sung by the public at [St Giles' Fair](/wiki/St_Giles%27_Fair "St Giles' Fair"), [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford"), 2007

The standard version of the melody and its key of G major are still those of the originally published version, although the start of the anthem is often signalled by an introductory timpani roll of two bars length. The bass line of the standard version differs little from the second voice part shown in the original, and there is a standard version in four-part harmony for choirs. The first three lines (six bars of music) are soft, ending with a short *crescendo* into "Send him victorious", and then is another *crescendo* at "over us:" into the final words "God save the King".

In the early-20th century, there existed a military band version in the higher key of B♭,[[36]](#cite_note-37) because it was easier for brass instruments to play in that key, though it had the disadvantage of being more difficult to sing; however, now most bands play it in the correct key of concert G.

Since 1953, the anthem is sometimes preceded by a fanfare composed by [Gordon Jacob](/wiki/Gordon_Jacob "Gordon Jacob") for the [coronation of Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Coronation_of_Queen_Elizabeth_II "Coronation of Queen Elizabeth II").[[37]](#cite_note-38)

##### O Lord Our God Arise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: O Lord Our God Arise")]

An additional stanza sung second was previously considered part of the standard lyrics in the UK:

> O Lord our God arise  
> Scatter his enemies  
> And make them fall  
> Confound their politics  
> Frustrate their knavish tricks  
> On thee our hopes we fix  
> God save us all

These lyrics appeared in some works of literature prior the coronation of Queen Elizabeth II, but only the version mentioned in the Standard Version in the United Kingdom was used at her Coronation, and ever since on all official occasions when two stanzas have been sung.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

There have been several attempts to rewrite the words. In the 19th century, there was some lively debate about the national anthem as verse two was considered by some to be slightly offensive in its use of the phrase "scatter her enemies". Some thought it placed better emphasis on the respective power of Parliament and the Crown to change "her enemies" to "our enemies"; others questioned the theology and proposed "thine enemies" instead. Sydney G. R. Coles wrote a completely new version, as did Canon F. K. Harford.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards200291-40)

According to Alan Michie's *The Crown and the People*, which was published in 1952, after the death of [King George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") but before the coronation of [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"), when the first [General Assembly of the United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly") was held in London in January 1946, the King, in honour of the occasion, "ordered the belligerent imperious second stanza of 'God Save the King' to be rewritten to bring it more into the spirit of the brotherhood of nations."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[40]](#cite_note-41)

##### William Hickson's alternative version

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: William Hickson's alternative version")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/St_Nicholas%2C_Charlwood%2C_trans-Atlantic_congratulations_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3617999.jpg/250px-St_Nicholas%2C_Charlwood%2C_trans-Atlantic_congratulations_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3617999.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Nicholas,_Charlwood,_trans-Atlantic_congratulations_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3617999.jpg)

In 1836, [William Edward Hickson](/wiki/William_Edward_Hickson "William Edward Hickson") wrote an alternative version, of which the first, third and fourth verses gained some currency when they were appended to the national anthem in *[The English Hymnal](/wiki/The_English_Hymnal "The English Hymnal")* (1906). His fourth verse was sung after the traditional first verse at [Queen Elizabeth II's Golden Jubilee National Service of Thanksgiving](/wiki/Golden_Jubilee_of_Elizabeth_II#Golden_Jubilee_Weekend "Golden Jubilee of Elizabeth II") in 2002, and during the raising of the [Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack") during the [2008 Summer Paralympics closing ceremony](/wiki/2008_Summer_Paralympics "2008 Summer Paralympics"), in which London took Paralympic flag from Beijing to host the [2012 Summer](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics") games. This verse is currently used as the final verse by the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland").[[41]](#cite_note-42)

> God bless our native land!  
> May Heav'n's protecting hand  
> Still guard our shore:

>

> With strength and righteousness:

>   
> Not in this land alone,  
> But be God's mercies known

> Lord make the nations see

##### Samuel Reynolds Hole's alternative version

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Samuel Reynolds Hole's alternative version")]

To mark the celebration of the [Diamond Jubilee of Queen Victoria](/wiki/Diamond_Jubilee_of_Queen_Victoria "Diamond Jubilee of Queen Victoria"), a modified version of the second verse was written by the [Dean of Rochester](/wiki/Dean_of_Rochester "Dean of Rochester"), the Very Reverend [Samuel Reynolds Hole](/wiki/Samuel_Hole "Samuel Hole"). A four-part harmony setting was then made by [Frederick Bridge](/wiki/Frederick_Bridge "Frederick Bridge"), and published by Novello.

> O Lord Our God Arise,  
> Scatter her enemies,  
> Make wars to cease;  
> Keep us from plague and dearth,  
> Turn thou our woes to mirth;  
> And over all the earth  
> Let there be peace.

The *[Musical Times](/wiki/Musical_Times "Musical Times")* commented: "There are some conservative minds who may regret the banishment of the 'knavish tricks' and aggressive spirit of the discarded verse, but it must be admitted that Dean Hole's lines are more consonant with the sentiment of modern Christianity." Others reactions were more negative, one report describing the setting as "unwarrantable liberties...worthy of the severest reprobation", with "too much of a Peace Society flavour about it...If we go about pleading for peace, other nations will get it into their heads that we are afraid of fighting." Perhaps unsurprisingly, Hole's version failed to replace the existing verse permanently.[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards2002[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_June_2023]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (June_2023)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-44)[[44]](#cite_note-45)[[45]](#cite_note-46)

> God save our land!  
>

#### Historic Jacobite and anti-Jacobite alternative verses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Historic Jacobite and anti-Jacobite alternative verses")]

Around 1745 anti-[Jacobite](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism") sentiment was captured in a verse appended to the song, with a prayer for the success of Field Marshal [George Wade](/wiki/George_Wade "George Wade")'s army then assembling at [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne"). These words attained some short-term use, although they did not appear in the published version in the October 1745 *Gentleman's Magazine*. This verse was first documented as an occasional addition to the original anthem by [Richard Clark](/wiki/Richard_Clark_(musician) "Richard Clark (musician)") in 1814,[[49]](#cite_note-51) and was also mentioned in a later article on the song, published by the *Gentleman's Magazine* in October 1836. Therein, it is presented as an "additional verse... though being of temporary application only... stored in the memory of an old friend... who was born in the very year 1745, and was thus the associate of those who heard it first sung", the lyrics given being:[[50]](#cite_note-clark-52)

> Lord, grant that Marshal Wade  
> May by thy mighty aid  
> Victory bring;  
> May he sedition hush,  
> and like a torrent rush  
> Rebellious Scots to crush!  
> God save the King!

The 1836 article and other sources make it clear that this verse was quickly abandoned after 1745 (Wade was replaced as Commander-in-Chief within a year following the Jacobite invasion of England), and it was certainly not used when the song became accepted as the British national anthem in the 1780s and 1790s.[[51]](#cite_note-53)[[52]](#cite_note-54) It was included as an integral part of the song in the *Oxford Book of Eighteenth-Century Verse* of 1926, although erroneously referencing the "fourth verse" to the *Gentleman's Magazine* article of 1745.[[53]](#cite_note-55)

On the opposing side, Jacobite beliefs were demonstrated in an alternative verse used during the same period:[[54]](#cite_note-56)

> God bless the prince, I pray,  
> God bless the prince, I pray,  
> [Charlie](/wiki/Charles_Edward_Stuart "Charles Edward Stuart") I mean;  
> That Scotland we may see  
> Freed from vile [Presbyt'ry](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism"),  
> Both [George](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain") and his [Feckie](/wiki/Frederick,_Prince_of_Wales "Frederick, Prince of Wales"),  
> Ever so, Amen.

In May 1800, following an attempt to assassinate [King George III](/wiki/George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom "George III of the United Kingdom") at the [Theatre Royal, Drury Lane](/wiki/Theatre_Royal,_Drury_Lane "Theatre Royal, Drury Lane"), in London, the playwright [Richard Sheridan](/wiki/Richard_Brinsley_Sheridan "Richard Brinsley Sheridan") immediately composed an additional verse, which was sung from the stage the same night:[[55]](#cite_note-57)[[56]](#cite_note-58)

> From every latent foe  
> From the assassin's blow  
> God save the King  
> O'er him Thine arm extend  
> For Britain's sake defend  
> Our father, king, and friend  
> God save the King!

Various other attempts were made during the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries to add verses to commemorate particular royal or national events. For example, according to Fitzroy Maclean, when Jacobite forces bypassed Wade's force and reached [Derby](/wiki/Derby "Derby"), but then retreated and when their garrison at [Carlisle Castle](/wiki/Carlisle_Castle "Carlisle Castle") surrendered to a second government army led by King George's son, the [Duke of Cumberland](/wiki/William_Augustus,_Duke_of_Cumberland "William Augustus, Duke of Cumberland"), another verse was added.[[57]](#cite_note-fn_1-59) Other short-lived verses were notably anti-French, such as the following, quoted in the book *Handel* by Edward J. Dent:[[58]](#cite_note-60)

> From France and Pretender  
> Great Britain defend her,  
> Foes let them fall;  
> From foreign slavery,  
> Priests and their knavery,  
> And Popish Reverie,  
> God save us all.

However, none of these additional verses survived into the 20th century.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards200290-61) Updated "full" versions including additional verses have been published more recently, including the standard three verses, Hickson's fourth verse, Sheridan's verse and the [Marshal Wade](/wiki/Marshal_Wade "Marshal Wade") verse.[[60]](#cite_note-62)[[61]](#cite_note-63)

#### Historic republican alternative

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Historic republican alternative")]

A version from 1794 composed by the American republican and French citizen [Joel Barlow](/wiki/Joel_Barlow "Joel Barlow")[[62]](#cite_note-64) celebrated the power of the [guillotine](/wiki/Guillotine "Guillotine") to liberate:[[63]](#cite_note-poetrywars-65)[[64]](#cite_note-66)

> God save the Guillotine  
> Till England's King and Queen  
> Her power shall prove:  
> Till each appointed knob  
> Affords a clipping job  
> Let no vile halter rob  
> *The Guillotine*  
>   
> France, let thy trumpet sound –  
> Tell all the world around  
> How [Capet](/wiki/House_of_Capet "House of Capet") fell;  
> And when great [George](/wiki/George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom "George III of the United Kingdom")'s poll  
> Shall in the basket roll,  
> Let mercy then control  
> *The Guillotine*  
>   
> When all the sceptre'd crew  
> Have paid their Homage, due  
> *The Guillotine*  
> Let Freedom's flag advance  
> Till all the world, like France  
> O'er tyrants' graves shall dance  
> And peace begin.

### Performance in the UK

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Performance in the UK")]

The style most commonly heard in official performances was proposed as the "proper interpretation" by King [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V"), who considered himself something of an expert (in view of the number of times he had heard it). An Army Order was duly issued in 1933, which laid down regulations for tempo, dynamics and orchestration. This included instructions such as that the opening "six bars will be played quietly by the reed band with horns and basses in a single phrase. Cornets and side-drum are to be added at the little scale-passage leading into the second half of the tune, and the full brass enters for the last eight bars". The official tempo for the opening section is a metronome setting of 60, with the second part played in a broader manner, at a metronome setting of 52.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScholes1970[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_June_2023]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (June_2023)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-67) In recent years the prescribed sombre-paced introduction is often played at a faster and livelier [tempo](/wiki/Tempo "Tempo").

Until the latter part of the 20th century, theatre and concert goers were expected to stand while the anthem was played after the conclusion of a show. In cinemas this brought a tendency for audiences to rush out while the end credits played to avoid this formality. (This can be seen in the 1972 *[Dad's Army](/wiki/Dad%27s_Army "Dad's Army")* episode "[A Soldier's Farewell](/wiki/A_Soldier%27s_Farewell "A Soldier's Farewell")".)

The anthem continues to be played at some traditional events such as [Wimbledon](/wiki/The_Championships,_Wimbledon "The Championships, Wimbledon"), [Royal Variety Performance](/wiki/Royal_Variety_Performance "Royal Variety Performance"), the [Edinburgh Tattoo](/wiki/Edinburgh_Tattoo "Edinburgh Tattoo"), [Royal Ascot](/wiki/Royal_Ascot "Royal Ascot"), [Henley Royal Regatta](/wiki/Henley_Royal_Regatta "Henley Royal Regatta") and [The Proms](/wiki/The_Proms "The Proms") as well as at royal events.

The anthem was traditionally played at close-down on the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), and with the introduction of commercial television to the UK this practice was adopted by some [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_channel) "ITV (TV channel)") companies (with the notable exceptions of [Granada](/wiki/Granada_Television "Granada Television"), [Thames Television](/wiki/Thames_Television "Thames Television"), [Central Television](/wiki/ITV_Central "ITV Central"), [Border Television](/wiki/ITV_Border "ITV Border") and [Yorkshire Television](/wiki/ITV_Yorkshire "ITV Yorkshire")). [BBC Two](/wiki/BBC_Two "BBC Two") also never played the anthem at close-down, and ITV dropped the practice in the late 1980s when the network switched to 24 hour broadcasting, but it continued on [BBC One](/wiki/BBC_One "BBC One") until 8 November 1997 (thereafter BBC One began to [simulcast](/wiki/Simulcast "Simulcast") with [BBC News](/wiki/BBC_News "BBC News") after end of programmes). The tradition is carried on, however, by [BBC Radio 4](/wiki/BBC_Radio_4 "BBC Radio 4"), which plays the anthem each night as a transition piece between the end of the Radio 4 broadcasting and the move to [BBC World Service](/wiki/BBC_World_Service "BBC World Service").[[66]](#cite_note-Guardian_on_Radio_4-68) BBC Radio 4 and [BBC Radio 2](/wiki/BBC_Radio_2 "BBC Radio 2") also play the National Anthem just before the 0700 and 0800 news bulletins on the actual and [official](/wiki/King%27s_Official_Birthday "King's Official Birthday") birthdays of the King and the birthdays of senior members of the [Royal Family](/wiki/Royal_Family "Royal Family"). On 17 January 2022 [GB News](/wiki/GB_News "GB News") started playing the anthem at 05:59 every morning at the beginning of the day's programming.[[67]](#cite_note-69)

The UK's national anthem usually prefaces the [royal Christmas message](/wiki/Royal_Christmas_message "Royal Christmas message") (although in 2007 it appeared at the end, taken from a recording of the 1957 television broadcast), and important royal announcements, such as of royal deaths, when it is played in a slower, sombre arrangement.

### Other British anthems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Other British anthems")]

Frequently, when an anthem is needed for one of the constituent [countries of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom") – at an international sporting event, for instance – an alternative song is used:

* England generally uses "God Save the King", but "[Jerusalem](/wiki/And_did_those_feet_in_ancient_time "And did those feet in ancient time")", "[Rule, Britannia!](/wiki/Rule,_Britannia! "Rule, Britannia!")" and "[Land of Hope and Glory](/wiki/Land_of_Hope_and_Glory "Land of Hope and Glory")" have also been used.[[68]](#cite_note-70)[[69]](#cite_note-71)
  + At international [test cricket](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") matches, [England](/wiki/England_cricket_team "England cricket team") has, since 2004, used "Jerusalem" as the anthem.[[70]](#cite_note-72)
  + At international [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") matches, [England](/wiki/England_national_rugby_league_team "England national rugby league team") uses "God Save the King" and also "Jerusalem".[[71]](#cite_note-73)
  + At international [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") and [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") matches, England uses "God Save the King".[[72]](#cite_note-74)
  + At the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"), [Team England](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_England "Commonwealth Games England") uses "Jerusalem" as their victory anthem.[[73]](#cite_note-75)
* Scotland uses "[Flower of Scotland](/wiki/Flower_of_Scotland "Flower of Scotland")" as their anthem for most sporting occasions.[[74]](#cite_note-76)
* Wales uses "[Hen Wlad Fy Nhadau](/wiki/Hen_Wlad_Fy_Nhadau "Hen Wlad Fy Nhadau")" ("Land of My Fathers") for governmental ceremonies and sporting occasions. At official occasions, especially those with royal connections, "God Save the King" is also played.[[75]](#cite_note-77)
* Northern Ireland uses "God Save the King" as its national anthem. However, many [Irish nationalists](/wiki/Irish_nationalism "Irish nationalism") feel unrepresented by the British anthem and seek an alternative.[[76]](#cite_note-78) Northern Ireland also uses the "[Londonderry Air](/wiki/Londonderry_Air "Londonderry Air")" as its victory anthem at the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games").[[77]](#cite_note-79) When sung, the "Londonderry Air" has the lyrics to "[Danny Boy](/wiki/Danny_Boy "Danny Boy")". At international [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") matches, where Northern Irish players compete alongside those from the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") as part of an [All-Ireland](/wiki/All-Ireland "All-Ireland") team, "[Ireland's Call](/wiki/Ireland%27s_Call "Ireland's Call")" is used.
* The [British and Irish Lions](/wiki/British_and_Irish_Lions "British and Irish Lions") rugby union tour of 2005 used the song "[The Power of Four](/wiki/The_Power_of_Four "The Power of Four")", but this experiment has not been repeated.[[78]](#cite_note-80)

The London 2012 Olympics Opening Ceremony provided a conscious use of three of the four anthems listed above; the ceremony began with a rendition of the first verse of "Jerusalem", before a choir in Northern Ireland sang "Danny Boy" and a choir in Edinburgh performed part of "Flower of Scotland". Notably, Wales was represented by the hymn "[Bread of Heaven](/wiki/Bread_of_Heaven "Bread of Heaven")", not "Hen Wlad Fy Nhadhau".

In April 2007 there was an [early day motion](/wiki/Early_day_motion "Early day motion"), number 1319, to the [British Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") to propose that there should be a separate England anthem: "That this House ... believes that all English sporting associations should adopt an appropriate song that English sportsmen and women, and the English public, would favour when competing as England". An amendment (EDM 1319A3) was proposed by [Evan Harris](/wiki/Evan_Harris "Evan Harris") of the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") that the song "should have a bit more oomph than *God Save The Queen* and should also not involve God."[[79]](#cite_note-81)

For more information see also:

* [National anthem of England](/wiki/National_anthem_of_England "National anthem of England")
* [National anthem of Scotland](/wiki/National_anthem_of_Scotland "National anthem of Scotland")
* [Hen Wlad Fy Nhadau](/wiki/Hen_Wlad_Fy_Nhadau "Hen Wlad Fy Nhadau")
* [National anthem of Northern Ireland](/wiki/National_anthem_of_Northern_Ireland "National anthem of Northern Ireland")

### Use in media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Use in media")]

On 3 November 2016, [Andrew Rosindell](/wiki/Andrew_Rosindell "Andrew Rosindell"), a [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") MP, argued in an [early day motion](/wiki/Early_day_motion "Early day motion") for a return to the broadcasting of the national anthem at the end of [BBC One](/wiki/BBC_One "BBC One") transmissions each day (the practice had been dropped in 1997, due to BBC One adopting 24-hour broadcasting by simulcasting [BBC News 24](/wiki/BBC_News_(British_TV_channel) "BBC News (British TV channel)") overnight, rendering [closedown](/wiki/Closedown "Closedown") obsolete),[[80]](#cite_note-82) to commemorate the [Brexit vote](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum") and [Britain's subsequent withdrawing](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") from the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"). At the evening of the same day, [BBC Two](/wiki/BBC_Two "BBC Two")'s *[Newsnight](/wiki/Newsnight "Newsnight")* programme ended its nightly broadcast with host of that night, [Kirsty Wark](/wiki/Kirsty_Wark "Kirsty Wark"), saying that they were "incredibly happy to oblige" Rosindell's request, and then played a clip of the [Sex Pistols](/wiki/Sex_Pistols "Sex Pistols")' [similarly named song](/wiki/God_Save_the_Queen_(Sex_Pistols_song) "God Save the Queen (Sex Pistols song)"), much to Rosindell's discontent.[[81]](#cite_note-metro-gstq-troll-83)

Since 18 January 2022, [GB News](/wiki/GB_News "GB News") has played the national anthem at the start of live programming every day.[[82]](#cite_note-84)[[83]](#cite_note-85)

## Use in other Commonwealth countries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Use in other Commonwealth countries")]

"God Save the King" was exported around the world via the expansion of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), serving as each country's national anthem. Throughout the [Empire's evolution](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations#History "Commonwealth of Nations") into the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), the song declined in use in most states which became independent. In New Zealand, it remains one of the official national anthems.[[84]](#cite_note-86)

### Antigua and Barbuda

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Antigua and Barbuda")]

Contrary to popular belief, "God Save The King" is not the royal anthem of Antigua and Barbuda in practice or officially. As of August 2025, there is no legislation in Antigua and Barbuda establishing a royal anthem, and during a royal visit in 2017, members of the royal family were greeted by the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* national anthem "[Fair Antigua, We Salute Thee](/wiki/Fair_Antigua,_We_Salute_Thee "Fair Antigua, We Salute Thee")". The Governor-General is also greeted by "Fair Antigua, We Salute Thee" in all formal circumstances.[[85]](#cite_note-87)[[86]](#cite_note-88)[[87]](#cite_note-89)[[88]](#cite_note-90)

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Australia")]

Further information: [Advance Australia Fair § History](/wiki/Advance_Australia_Fair#History "Advance Australia Fair")

In Australia "God Save the King" was declared as the royal anthem on 27 October 2022, replacing the previous declaration of "God Save the Queen" as the royal anthem on 19 April 1984.[[89]](#cite_note-91) It declares that the song is to be played when the [monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Australia "Monarchy of Australia") or a member of the royal family is present. The [Australian Government](/wiki/Australian_Government "Australian Government") also advises that when the King is in Australia, the royal anthem is played at the beginning of an event and the national anthem, "[Advance Australia Fair](/wiki/Advance_Australia_Fair "Advance Australia Fair")", is to be played at the end.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-9)

Prior to 1974, "God Save the Queen" was the national anthem of Australia. It was replaced that year with "Advance Australia Fair" by the Labor [Whitlam government](/wiki/Whitlam_government "Whitlam government"). Following the elevation of the Liberal [Fraser government](/wiki/Fraser_government "Fraser government"), "God Save the Queen" was restored as the national anthem in 1976 alongside three other "national songs". A [plebiscite held in 1977](/wiki/1977_Australian_plebiscite_(National_Song) "1977 Australian plebiscite (National Song)") preferred "Advance Australia Fair" as the exclusive "national song", to exist alongside the national anthem of "God Save the Queen". The subsequent Labor [Hawke government](/wiki/Hawke_government "Hawke government") later advised the proclamation of "Advance Australia Fair" as the national anthem in 1984, with "God Save the Queen" redesignated as the royal anthem.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[90]](#cite_note-92)

### Belize

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Belize")]

"God Save the King" is the royal anthem of Belize.[[91]](#cite_note-The_World_Factbook-93) The viceregal salute to the [Belizean governor-general](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Belize "Governor-General of Belize") is composed of the first verse of "God Save the King" and the chorus of national anthem, "[Land of the Free](/wiki/Land_of_the_Free_(anthem) "Land of the Free (anthem)")".[[92]](#cite_note-94)

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Canada")]

Further information: [Canadian royal symbols § Verbal and musical symbols](/wiki/Canadian_royal_symbols#Verbal_and_musical_symbols "Canadian royal symbols"), and [Canadian patriotic music](/wiki/Canadian_patriotic_music "Canadian patriotic music")

Percival Price performs "[O Canada](/wiki/O_Canada "O Canada")" and "God Save the King" on the [Peace Tower](/wiki/Peace_Tower "Peace Tower") [Carillon](/wiki/Carillon "Carillon"), 1927.

By [convention](/wiki/Convention_(norm) "Convention (norm)")[[93]](#cite_note-DCH-95) "God Save the King" (French: *Dieu Sauve le Roi*, *Dieu Sauve la Reine* when a Queen) is the royal anthem of Canada.[[94]](#cite_note-96)[[95]](#cite_note-CE-97)[[96]](#cite_note-98)[[97]](#cite_note-99)[[98]](#cite_note-100) It is sometimes played or sung together with the national anthem, "[O Canada](/wiki/O_Canada "O Canada")", at private and public events organised by groups such as the [Government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada"), the [Royal Canadian Legion](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Legion "Royal Canadian Legion"), police services and loyal groups.[[99]](#cite_note-101)[[100]](#cite_note-102)[[101]](#cite_note-103)[[102]](#cite_note-104)[[103]](#cite_note-105) The [governor general](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") and provincial lieutenant governors are accorded the "Viceregal Salute", comprising the first three lines of "God Save the King", followed by the first and last lines of "O Canada".[[104]](#cite_note-106)

"God Save the King" has been sung in Canada since the late 1700s and by the mid-20th century was, along with "O Canada", one of the country's two *de facto* national anthems, the first and last verses of the standard British version being used.[[105]](#cite_note-QHE-107) By-laws and practices governing the use of either song during public events in municipalities varied; in Toronto, "God Save the King" was employed, while in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") it was "O Canada". Prime Minister [Lester B. Pearson](/wiki/Lester_B._Pearson "Lester B. Pearson") in 1964 said one song would have to be chosen as the country's national anthem and, three years later, he advised Governor General [Georges Vanier](/wiki/Georges_Vanier "Georges Vanier") to appoint the Special Joint Committee of the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada") and [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada") on the National and Royal Anthems. Within two months, on 12 April 1967, the committee presented its conclusion that "God Save the Queen" (as this was during the reign of Queen Elizabeth II), whose music and lyrics were found to be in the [public domain](/wiki/Public_domain "Public domain"),[[106]](#cite_note-DCHOCanada-108) should be designated as the royal anthem of Canada and "O Canada" as the national anthem, one verse from each, in [both official languages](/wiki/Official_bilingualism_in_Canada "Official bilingualism in Canada"), to be adopted by parliament. The group was then charged with establishing official lyrics for each song; for "God Save the Queen", the English words were those inherited from the United Kingdom and the French words were taken from those that had been adopted in 1952 for the [coronation of Elizabeth II](/wiki/Coronation_of_Elizabeth_II "Coronation of Elizabeth II").[[95]](#cite_note-CE-97) When the bill pronouncing "O Canada" as the national anthem was put through parliament, the joint committee's earlier recommendations regarding "God Save the Queen" were not included.[[106]](#cite_note-DCHOCanada-108)

The [Department of National Defence](/wiki/Department_of_National_Defence_(Canada) "Department of National Defence (Canada)") and the [Canadian Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Forces "Canadian Forces") regulates that "God Save the King" be played as a salute to the [monarch of Canada](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada") and other members of the [Canadian royal family](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada#Royal_family_and_house "Monarchy of Canada"),[[107]](#cite_note-DND-109) though it may also be used as a hymn or prayer. The words are not to be sung when the song is played as a military royal salute and is abbreviated to the first three lines, while arms are being presented.[[107]](#cite_note-DND-109) Elizabeth II stipulated that the arrangement in G major by Lieutenant Colonel Basil H. Brown be used in Canada. The authorised version to be played by [pipe bands](/wiki/Pipe_band "Pipe band") is *Mallorca*.[[107]](#cite_note-DND-109)

#### Lyrics in Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Lyrics in Canada")]

"God Save the King" has been translated into French,[[108]](#cite_note-110) but this translation does not fit the music and cannot be sung. Nevertheless, this translation has been adapted into a bilingual version that can be sung when the monarch is male, and has been sung during public ceremonies, such as the National [Remembrance Day](/wiki/Remembrance_Day "Remembrance Day") Ceremony at the [National War Memorial](/wiki/National_War_Memorial_(Canada) "National War Memorial (Canada)") in [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa"):[[109]](#cite_note-111)

> Dieu sauve notre Roi,  
> Notre gracieux Roi,  
> Vive le Roi!  
> Send him victorious,  
> Happy and glorious;  
> Long to reign over us,  
> God save the King!

A special singable one-verse adaptation[[110]](#cite_note-112) is used when a singable French version is required, such as when royalty is present at an official occasion:

> Dieu sauve notre Roi!  
> Notre gracieux Roi!  
> Vive le Roi!  
> Rends-lui victorieux,  
> Heureux et glorieux,  
> Que soit long son règne sur nous,  
> Vive le Roi!

There is a special Canadian verse in English which was once commonly sung in addition to the two standing verses:[[105]](#cite_note-QHE-107)

> Our loved Dominion bless  
> With peace and happiness  
> From shore to shore;  
> And let our Empire be  
> Loyal, united, free,  
> True to herself and Thee  
> For evermore.

### Channel Islands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Channel Islands")]

"God Save the King" is used by both Bailiwicks of the [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands") as an alternative to their respective national anthems. Its use case and popular version is generally similar to how it is used in the United Kingdom. However, the anthem has been translated in [Jèrriais](/wiki/J%C3%A8rriais "Jèrriais"):[[111]](#cite_note-113): 35

> Dgieu sauve not' Duc,  
> Longue vie à not' Duc,  
> Dgieu sauve la Rei!  
> Rends-la victorieuse  
> Jouaiyeuse et glorieuse;  
> Qu'on règne sus nous heûtheuse –  
> Dgieu sauve la Rei!  
>   
> Tes dons les pus précieux,  
> Sus yi vèrse des cieux,  
> Dgieu sauve la Rei!  
> Qu'on défende nous louais  
> Et d'un tchoeu et d'eune vouaix  
> Jé chantons à janmais  
> Dgieu sauve la Rei!

The meaning is broadly similar to the first paragraph of the English version, except for the first two lines which say "God save our [Duke](/wiki/Sovereign_in_right_of_the_Bailiwick_of_Jersey "Sovereign in right of the Bailiwick of Jersey")" and "Long live our Duke".

### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

Further information: [National anthems of New Zealand](/wiki/National_anthems_of_New_Zealand "National anthems of New Zealand")

New Zealand inherited "God Save the King" as its anthem, which served as the sole national anthem until 1977, when "[God Defend New Zealand](/wiki/God_Defend_New_Zealand "God Defend New Zealand")" was introduced as a second. Since then, "God Save the King" is most often only played when the sovereign, [governor-general](/wiki/Governor-General_of_New_Zealand "Governor-General of New Zealand")[[112]](#cite_note-cryer-114) or other member of the Royal Family is present, or on some occasions such as [Anzac Day](/wiki/Anzac_Day "Anzac Day").[[113]](#cite_note-115)[[114]](#cite_note-AnthemProtocol-116) The [Māori-language](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language") version was written by [Edward Marsh Williams](/wiki/Edward_Marsh_Williams "Edward Marsh Williams") under the title, "*E te atua tohungia te kuini*".[[115]](#cite_note-te_reo-117)

There is a special New Zealand verse in English which was once commonly sung to replace the second and third verses:[[116]](#cite_note-history-god-save-queen-118)

> Not on this land alone  
> But be God's mercies known  
> From shore to shore.  
> Lord, make the nations see  
> That we in liberty  
> Should form one family  
> The wide world o'er.

#### Lyrics in Māori

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Lyrics in Māori")]

All verses of "God Save the King" have been translated into the Māori language.[[115]](#cite_note-te_reo-117) The three verses is shown below:

> Me tohu e te Atua  
> To matou Kīngi pai:  
> Kia ora ia  
> Meinga kia maia ia,  
> Kia hari nui, kia koa,  
> Kia kingi tonu ia,  
> Tau tini noa.  
>   
> Ko ona hoa whawhai,  
> Kia kore maia mai,  
> Kia whati noa.  
> Me whakaruru mai  
> A ratou hui e koe;   
> To matou Kīngi pai   
> Tau tini noa.  
>   
> Nga tino mea papai,   
> Me tuku mai e koe   
> Māna katoa.   
> Ko ia kira kuini roa,   
> Hei take mo te koa,   
> E mapu ai te reo   
> Kia ora ia.

### Rhodesia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Rhodesia")]

When [Rhodesia](/wiki/Rhodesia "Rhodesia") issued its [Unilateral Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Rhodesia%27s_Unilateral_Declaration_of_Independence "Rhodesia's Unilateral Declaration of Independence") from the UK on 11 November 1965, it did so while still maintaining loyalty to Queen Elizabeth II as the [Rhodesian head of state](/wiki/Queen_of_Rhodesia "Queen of Rhodesia"), despite the non-recognition of the Rhodesian government by the United Kingdom and the United Nations;[[117]](#cite_note-119) "God Save the Queen" therefore remained the Rhodesian national anthem. This was supposed to demonstrate the continued allegiance of the Rhodesian people to the monarch, but the retention in Rhodesia of a song so associated with the UK while the two countries were at loggerheads regarding its constitutional status caused Rhodesian state occasions to have "a faintly ironic tone", in the words of *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")*. Nevertheless, "God Save the Queen" remained Rhodesia's national anthem until March 1970, when it formally declared itself a republic.[[118]](#cite_note-buch243-120) "[Rise, O Voices of Rhodesia](/wiki/Rise,_O_Voices_of_Rhodesia "Rise, O Voices of Rhodesia")" was adopted in its stead in 1974 and remained in use until the country returned to the UK's control in December 1979.[[119]](#cite_note-121)[[120]](#cite_note-122) Since the internationally recognised independence of the Republic of [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe") in April 1980, "God Save the King" has had no official status there.[[121]](#cite_note-123)

### Saint Vincent and the Grenadines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Saint Vincent and the Grenadines")]

"God Save the King" is the royal anthem of Saint Vincent and the Grenadines. It is played on royal and vice-regal occasions. The Vice-Regal Salute to the governor general is composed of the chorus of "God Save the King" and followed by that of the National Anthem, "[Saint Vincent, Land so Beautiful](/wiki/Saint_Vincent,_Land_so_Beautiful "Saint Vincent, Land so Beautiful")".[[5]](#cite_note-SVG-5)

All proclamations in Saint Vincent and the Grenadines end with the phrase: "God Save the King".[[122]](#cite_note-124)

### South Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: South Africa")]

"God Save the King" ([Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans_language "Afrikaans language"): *God Red die Koning*, *God Red die Koningin* when a Queen) was a co-national anthem of [South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa") from 1938 until 1957,[[123]](#cite_note-DieStem-125) when it was formally replaced by "*[Die Stem van Suid-Afrika](/wiki/Die_Stem_van_Suid-Afrika "Die Stem van Suid-Afrika")*" as the sole national anthem.[[123]](#cite_note-DieStem-125) The latter served as a sort of *de facto* co-national anthem alongside the former until 1938.[[123]](#cite_note-DieStem-125)

## Use elsewhere

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Use elsewhere")]

The melody has often been used, with lyrics slightly or significantly altered, for royal or national anthems of other countries.

During the 19th century, it was used officially in Sweden,[[124]](#cite_note-126)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[c]](#cite_note-127) and in Iceland.[[125]](#cite_note-128)[[d]](#cite_note-129) It was also in official usage for brief periods in [Imperial Russia](/wiki/Imperial_Russia "Imperial Russia"),[[e]](#cite_note-130) in [Greece](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Greece "Kingdom of Greece"),[[126]](#cite_note-131) [Siam](/wiki/Rattanakosin_Kingdom_(1782%E2%80%931932) "Rattanakosin Kingdom (1782–1932)")[[f]](#cite_note-132) and in the [Kingdom of Hawaii](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hawaii "Kingdom of Hawaii").[[127]](#cite_note-133) It remains as the [royal anthem of Norway](/wiki/Kongesangen "Kongesangen") ("Gud sign vår Konge god").

In Germany, it was used by the kingdoms of Prussia, Hanover, Saxony and [Bavaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bavaria "Kingdom of Bavaria"), and was adopted as anthem of the German emperor ("[Heil dir im Siegerkranz](/wiki/Heil_dir_im_Siegerkranz "Heil dir im Siegerkranz")") after unification in 1871; however, it lacked the support necessary to become the official anthem of the empire.[[128]](#cite_note-134) It remains as the [national anthem of Liechtenstein](/wiki/National_anthem_of_Liechtenstein "National anthem of Liechtenstein"), and was used by Switzerland until 1961 ("Rufst du, mein Vaterland").

In Latvia, it was used by Latvians for the patriotic song "Dievs, svētī Kurzemi/Vidzemi!" ("God bless Kurzeme/Vidzeme!", depending on the region it was used in) in the 19th century.[[129]](#cite_note-135)
