### Composers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Composers")]

About 140 composers have used the tune in their compositions.[[6]](#cite_note-royal_family-7)

[Ludwig van Beethoven](/wiki/Ludwig_van_Beethoven "Ludwig van Beethoven") composed a set of seven piano variations in the key of C major to the theme of "God Save the King", catalogued as [WoO](/wiki/WoO "WoO") 78 (1802–1803). He also quotes it in his orchestral work *[Wellington's Victory](/wiki/Wellington%27s_Victory "Wellington's Victory")*. It is also the first song arranged in the collection WoO 157.

[Muzio Clementi](/wiki/Muzio_Clementi "Muzio Clementi") used the theme to "God Save the King" in his [Symphony](/wiki/Symphony "Symphony") No. 3 in G major, often called the "Great National Symphony", catalogued as WoO 34. Clementi paid a high tribute to his adopted homeland (the United Kingdom) where he grew up and stayed most of his lifetime. He based the symphony (about 1816–1824) on "God Save the King", which is hinted at earlier in the work, not least in the second movement, and announced by the trombones in the finale.

[Johann Christian Bach](/wiki/Johann_Christian_Bach "Johann Christian Bach") composed a set of variations on "God Save the King" for the finale to his sixth keyboard concerto (Op. 1) written c. 1763.

[Joseph Haydn](/wiki/Joseph_Haydn "Joseph Haydn") was impressed by the use of "God Save the King" as a national anthem during his visit to London in 1794, and on his return to Austria composed a different tune, "[Gott erhalte Franz den Kaiser](/wiki/Gott_erhalte_Franz_den_Kaiser "Gott erhalte Franz den Kaiser")" ("God Save Emperor Francis"), for the birthday of the last [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor") and [Roman-German King](/wiki/King_of_the_Romans "King of the Romans"), [Francis II](/wiki/Francis_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Francis II, Holy Roman Emperor"), which became the basis for the anthem of the later [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire"), and ultimately for the [German national anthem](/wiki/German_national_anthem "German national anthem").

[Franz Liszt](/wiki/Franz_Liszt "Franz Liszt") wrote a piano paraphrase on the anthem (S.259 in the official catalogue, c. 1841).

[Johann Strauss I](/wiki/Johann_Strauss_I "Johann Strauss I") quoted "God Save the Queen" in full at the end of his [waltz](/wiki/Waltz "Waltz") "*Huldigung der Königin Victoria von Grossbritannien*" (Homage to Queen Victoria of Great Britain), Op. 103, where he also quoted "[Rule, Britannia!](/wiki/Rule,_Britannia! "Rule, Britannia!")" in full at the beginning of the piece.

[Siegfried August Mahlmann](/wiki/Siegfried_August_Mahlmann "Siegfried August Mahlmann") in the early 19th century wrote alternate lyrics to adapt the hymn for the [Kingdom of Saxony](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Saxony "Kingdom of Saxony"), as "*Gott segne Sachsenland*" ("God Bless Saxony").[[130]](#cite_note-136)

[Christian Heinrich Rinck](/wiki/Christian_Heinrich_Rinck "Christian Heinrich Rinck") wrote two sets of variations on the anthem: the last movement of his Piano Trio, Op. 34, No. 1 (1815) is a set of five variations and a concluding coda; and Theme (Andante) and (12) Variations in C major on "*Heil dir im Siegerkranz*" (God Save the King), Op. 55.

[Heinrich Marschner](/wiki/Heinrich_Marschner "Heinrich Marschner") used the anthem in his "*Grande Ouverture solennelle*", Op. 78 (1842).

[Gaetano Donizetti](/wiki/Gaetano_Donizetti "Gaetano Donizetti") used this anthem in his opera "[Roberto Devereux](/wiki/Roberto_Devereux "Roberto Devereux")".

[Joachim Raff](/wiki/Joachim_Raff "Joachim Raff") used this anthem in his Jubelouverture, Op. 103 (1864) dedicated to [Adolf, Duke of Nassau](/wiki/Adolf,_Duke_of_Nassau "Adolf, Duke of Nassau"), on the 25th anniversary of his reign.

[Gioachino Rossini](/wiki/Gioachino_Rossini "Gioachino Rossini") used this anthem in the last scene of his *[Il viaggio a Reims](/wiki/Il_viaggio_a_Reims "Il viaggio a Reims")*, when all the characters, coming from many different European countries, sing a song which recalls their own homeland. Lord Sidney, bass, sings "*Della real pianta*" on the notes of "God Save the King". [Samuel Ramey](/wiki/Samuel_Ramey "Samuel Ramey") used to interpolate a spectacular virtuoso [cadenza](/wiki/Cadenza "Cadenza") at the end of the song.

[Fernando Sor](/wiki/Fernando_Sor "Fernando Sor") used the anthem in his 12 Studies, Op. 6: No. 10 in C major in the section marked 'Maestoso.'

[Arthur Sullivan](/wiki/Arthur_Sullivan "Arthur Sullivan") quotes the anthem at the end of his ballet *[Victoria and Merrie England](/wiki/Victoria_and_Merrie_England "Victoria and Merrie England")*.

[Claude Debussy](/wiki/Claude_Debussy "Claude Debussy") opens with a brief introduction of "God Save the King" in one of his [Preludes](/wiki/Preludes_(Debussy) "Preludes (Debussy)"), *[Hommage à S. Pickwick Esq. P.P.M.P.C.](/wiki/Hommage_%C3%A0_S._Pickwick_Esq._P.P.M.P.C. "Hommage à S. Pickwick Esq. P.P.M.P.C.")*. The piece draws its inspiration from the main character of the [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens") novel *[The Pickwick Papers](/wiki/The_Pickwick_Papers "The Pickwick Papers")*.

[Niccolò Paganini](/wiki/Niccol%C3%B2_Paganini "Niccolò Paganini") wrote a set of highly virtuosic variations on "God Save the King" as his Op. 9.

[Max Reger](/wiki/Max_Reger "Max Reger") wrote *Variations and Fugue on '*Heil dir im Siegerkranz*' (God Save the King)* for organ in 1901 after the death of [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria"). It does not have an opus number.

A week before the Coronation Ode was due to be premiered at the June 1902 "Coronation Gala Concert" at [Covent Garden](/wiki/Covent_Garden "Covent Garden") (it was cancelled, owing to the King's illness), Sir [Edward Elgar](/wiki/Edward_Elgar "Edward Elgar") introduced an arrangement of "Land of Hope and Glory" as a solo song performed by [Clara Butt](/wiki/Clara_Butt "Clara Butt") at a "Coronation Concert" at the Albert Hall. Novello seized upon the prevailing patriotism and requested that Elgar arrange the National Anthem as an appropriate opening for a concert performed in front of the Court and numerous British and foreign dignitaries. This version for orchestra and chorus, which is enlivened by use of *a cappella* and marcato effects, was also performed at the opening of the British Empire Exhibition at Wembley on [St. George's Day](/wiki/St._George%27s_Day "St. George's Day"), 1924, and recorded under the composer's baton in 1928, with the London Symphony Orchestra and the Philharmonic Choir.[[131]](#cite_note-137) Elgar also used the first verse of the anthem as the climax of a short "Civic Procession and Anthem", written to accompany the mayoral procession at the opening of the Hereford Music Festival on 4 September 1927. This premiere performance was recorded, and is today available on CD; the score was lost following the festival, and Elgar reconstructed it by ear from the recording.[[132]](#cite_note-138)

[Carl Maria von Weber](/wiki/Carl_Maria_von_Weber "Carl Maria von Weber") uses the "God Save the King" theme at the end of his "Jubel Overture".

[Giuseppe Verdi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Verdi "Giuseppe Verdi") included "God Save the Queen" in his "*[Inno delle nazioni](/wiki/Inno_delle_nazioni "Inno delle nazioni")*" (Hymn of the Nations), composed for the London [1862 International Exhibition](/wiki/1862_International_Exhibition "1862 International Exhibition").

[Benjamin Britten](/wiki/Benjamin_Britten "Benjamin Britten") arranged "God Save the Queen" in 1961 for the [Leeds Festival](/wiki/Leeds_Festival_(classical_music) "Leeds Festival (classical music)"). This version has been programmed several times at the [Last Night of the Proms](/wiki/Last_Night_of_the_Proms "Last Night of the Proms").[[133]](#cite_note-139)

[Charles Ives](/wiki/Charles_Ives "Charles Ives") wrote *[Variations on "America"](/wiki/Variations_on_%22America%22 "Variations on \"America\"")* for organ in 1891 at age seventeen. It included a polytonal section in three simultaneous keys, though this was omitted from performances at his father's request, because "it made the boys laugh out loud". Ives was fond of the rapid pedal line in the final variation, which he said was "almost as much fun as playing baseball". The piece was not published until 1949; the final version includes an introduction, seven variations and a polytonal interlude. The piece was adapted for orchestra in 1963 by [William Schuman](/wiki/William_Schuman "William Schuman"). This version became popular during the bicentennial celebrations, and is often heard at pops concerts.

[Muthuswami Dikshitar](/wiki/Muthuswami_Dikshitar "Muthuswami Dikshitar") (1776–1835), one of the musical trinity in South Indian classical ([Carnatic](/wiki/Carnatic_music "Carnatic music")) music composed some [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") pieces set to Western tunes. These are in the raga [Sankarabharanam](/wiki/Dheerasankarabharanam "Dheerasankarabharanam") and are referred to as "*nottu swaras*". Among these, the composition "*Santatam Pahimam Sangita Shyamale*" is set to the tune of "God Save the Queen".

[Sigismond Thalberg](/wiki/Sigismond_Thalberg "Sigismond Thalberg") (1812–1871), Swiss composer and one of the most famous virtuoso pianists of the 19th century, wrote a [fantasia](/wiki/Fantasia_(music) "Fantasia (music)") on "God Save the Queen".

[Johan Nepomuk Hummel](/wiki/Johan_Nepomuk_Hummel "Johan Nepomuk Hummel") (1778–1837) wrote *Variations on God Save the King in D major*, Op. 10 and quoted the tune briefly in his *Freudenfest-Ouverture in D major*, S 148.

[Jan Ladislav Dussek](/wiki/Jan_Ladislav_Dussek "Jan Ladislav Dussek") wrote a set of theme with 5 variations for piano on God Save the King.[[134]](#cite_note-140)

[Adolphe Blanc](/wiki/Adolphe_Blanc "Adolphe Blanc") wrote a set of variations for [piano six hands](/wiki/Piano_six_hands "Piano six hands") on this theme.[[135]](#cite_note-141)

[Adrien-François Servais](/wiki/Adrien-Fran%C3%A7ois_Servais "Adrien-François Servais") (1807–66) and [Joseph Ghys](/w/index.php?title=Joseph_Ghys&action=edit&redlink=1 "Joseph Ghys (page does not exist)") (1801–48) wrote *Variations brillantes et concertantes sur l'air "God Save the King"*, Op. 38, for violin and cello and performed it in London and St Petersburg.[[136]](#cite_note-142)

[Georges Onslow](/wiki/Georges_Onslow "Georges Onslow") (1784–1853) used the tune in his String Quartet No. 7 in G minor, Op. 9, second movement.

[Hans Huber](/wiki/Hans_Huber_(composer) "Hans Huber (composer)") used the melody ("*[Rufst du, mein Vaterland](/wiki/Rufst_du,_mein_Vaterland "Rufst du, mein Vaterland")*") in the first movement of his Symphony no 3 in C minor, Op. 118 ("Heroic").

[Ferdinando Carulli](/wiki/Ferdinando_Carulli "Ferdinando Carulli") used the melody in *Fantaisie sur un air national anglais*, for recorder & guitar, Op. 102.

[Louis Drouet](/wiki/Louis_Drouet "Louis Drouet") composed "Variations on the air God save the King" for flute and piano.

[Gordon Jacob](/wiki/Gordon_Jacob "Gordon Jacob") wrote a choral arrangement of "God Save the Queen" with a trumpet fanfare introduction, for the [coronation of Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Coronation_of_Elizabeth_II "Coronation of Elizabeth II") in 1953.[[137]](#cite_note-143)

### Rock adaptations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Rock adaptations")]

[Jimi Hendrix](/wiki/Jimi_Hendrix "Jimi Hendrix") played an impromptu version of "God Save the Queen" to open his set at the [Isle of Wight Festival 1970](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight_Festival_1970 "Isle of Wight Festival 1970"). Just before walking onto the stage, he asked "How does it [the anthem] go again?". Hendrix gave the same sort of distortion and improvisation of "God Save the Queen", as he had done with "[The Star-Spangled Banner](/wiki/The_Star-Spangled_Banner "The Star-Spangled Banner")" at the [Woodstock](/wiki/Woodstock "Woodstock") Festival in 1969.[[138]](#cite_note-HOPK-144)

[Queen](/wiki/Queen_(band) "Queen (band)") recorded an instrumental version of "God Save the Queen" for their 1975 album *[A Night at the Opera](/wiki/A_Night_at_the_Opera_(Queen_album) "A Night at the Opera (Queen album)")*. Guitarist [Brian May](/wiki/Brian_May "Brian May") adapted the melody using layers of [overdubbed](/wiki/Overdub "Overdub") electric guitars, a technique he also used on many other Queen songs. This recorded version was played at the end of every Queen concert from the end of 1974 to 1986, while the members of the band took their bows. On Queen's [Magic Tour](/wiki/Magic_Tour_(Queen) "Magic Tour (Queen)") in 1986, vocalist [Freddie Mercury](/wiki/Freddie_Mercury "Freddie Mercury") would leave the stage shortly before the end of the show, and return wearing a crown and cloak as "God Save the Queen" played.[[139]](#cite_note-145) On 3 June 2002, during the [Queen's Golden Jubilee](/wiki/Golden_Jubilee_of_Elizabeth_II "Golden Jubilee of Elizabeth II"), May performed the anthem on his [Red Special](/wiki/Red_Special "Red Special") electric guitar for [Party at the Palace](/wiki/Party_at_the_Palace "Party at the Palace") from the roof of [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace"). Footage of this performance appears on a DVD that was included 30th Anniversary edition of *A Night at the Opera*.[[140]](#cite_note-146)

In 1977, the [Sex Pistols](/wiki/Sex_Pistols "Sex Pistols") recorded a song titled "[God Save the Queen](/wiki/God_Save_the_Queen_(Sex_Pistols_song) "God Save the Queen (Sex Pistols song)")" in open reference to the national anthem and the [Queen's Silver Jubilee celebrations](/wiki/Silver_Jubilee_of_Elizabeth_II "Silver Jubilee of Elizabeth II") that year, with the song intending to stand for sympathy for the working class and resentment of the monarchy.[[141]](#cite_note-SPIST-147) They were banned from many venues, censored by mainstream media, and reached number 2 on the official UK singles charts and number 1 on the NME chart.[[141]](#cite_note-SPIST-147)[[142]](#cite_note-148)

A version of "God Save the Queen" by the [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") band [Madness](/wiki/Madness_(band) "Madness (band)") features the melody of the song played on [kazoos](/wiki/Kazoo "Kazoo"). It was included on the compilation album *[The Business – the Definitive Singles Collection](/wiki/The_Business_%E2%80%93_the_Definitive_Singles_Collection "The Business – the Definitive Singles Collection")*.[[143]](#cite_note-149)

### Computer music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Computer music")]

The anthem was the first piece of music played on a computer, and the first computer music to be recorded.

Musical notes were first generated by a computer programmed by [Alan Turing](/wiki/Alan_Turing "Alan Turing") at the Computing Machine Laboratory of the [University of Manchester](/wiki/University_of_Manchester "University of Manchester") in 1948. The first music proper, a performance of the National Anthem was programmed by [Christopher Strachey](/wiki/Christopher_Strachey "Christopher Strachey") on the Mark II [Manchester Electronic Computer](/wiki/Manchester_Electronic_Computer "Manchester Electronic Computer") at same venue, in 1951. Later that year, short extracts of three pieces, the first being the National Anthem, were recorded there by a [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") outside broadcasting unit: the other pieces being "[Baa Baa Black Sheep](/wiki/Baa_Baa_Black_Sheep "Baa Baa Black Sheep")" and "[In the Mood](/wiki/In_the_Mood "In the Mood")". Researchers at the [University of Canterbury](/wiki/University_of_Canterbury "University of Canterbury"), Christchurch restored the acetate master disc in 2016 and the results may be heard on [SoundCloud](/wiki/SoundCloud "SoundCloud").[[144]](#cite_note-Turing-150)[[145]](#cite_note-BL-2016-09-151)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Reception")]

The philosopher and reformer [Jeremy Bentham](/wiki/Jeremy_Bentham "Jeremy Bentham") praised "God Save the King" in 1796: "the melody recommending itself by beauty to the most polished ears, and by its simplicity to the rudest ear. A song of this complexion, implanted by the habit of half a century in the mass of popular sentiment, can not be refused a place in the inventory of the national blessings."[[146]](#cite_note-152) Ludwig van Beethoven wrote "I have to show the English a little of what a blessing 'God Save the King' is".[[147]](#cite_note-153)

### Calls for a new national anthem(s)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=God_Save_the_King&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Calls for a new national anthem(s)")]

There have been calls within the UK for a new national anthem, whether it be for the United Kingdom itself, Britain or England (which all currently use "God Save the King"). There are many reasons people cite for wishing for a new national anthem, such as: from a [secular](/wiki/Secularity "Secularity") standpoint,[[148]](#cite_note-154) claims of God Save the King" being long outdated and irrelevant in the 21st century,[[149]](#cite_note-155) rejection of odes to promoting war and [imperialism](/wiki/Imperialism "Imperialism")[[150]](#cite_note-156) and rejection of praising the monarchy from a [republican](/wiki/Republicanism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Republicanism in the United Kingdom") perspective.[[151]](#cite_note-157) A further reason is that England has no anthem of its own for sporting contests and the like, whereas Scotland, Northern Ireland and Wales have unofficial anthems—"[Flower of Scotland](/wiki/Flower_of_Scotland "Flower of Scotland")", "[Londonderry Air](/wiki/Londonderry_Air "Londonderry Air")" and "[Hen Wlad Fy Nhadau](/wiki/Hen_Wlad_Fy_Nhadau "Hen Wlad Fy Nhadau")"—while England tends to use "God Save the King" exclusively and also unofficially.
